## reference · ux skeleton screens perceived loading

* Use skeleton screens to indicate full-page loads and reduce perceived loading time.
* There are three main types of skeleton screens:
  * Static-content and -image skeleton screens (most common, wireframe-like)
  * Animated skeleton screens (with pulsating movement or gradients)
  * Frame-display skeleton screens (not recommended, displays only frame of application)
* Benefits of skeleton screens:
  * Prevent users from thinking the site isn't working
  * Create the illusion of a shorter wait time
  * Reduce users' cognitive load
* Guidelines for using skeleton screens:
  * Use for wait times under 10 seconds
  * Use spinners or wait animations for single module loads (2-10 seconds)
  * Use progress bars for wait times over 10 seconds
  * Don't use skeleton screens or spinners for page loads under 1 second
* Best practices:
  * Avoid frame-display skeleton screens
  * Use skeleton screens to indicate progress only for full-page loads
  * Don't use skeleton screens for process-related indicators (e.g. downloads, uploads)

Sources: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/skeleton-screens/
